business_info: In internal screening dashboards, molecules with unusually skewed elemental composition are sometimes flagged using a simple inorganic-to-organic atom ratio, but this is only a heuristic and is not directly tied to regulatory classifications.

For exploratory QSAR work, scientists occasionally define “metallic enrichment” scores by counting all atoms whose elemental symbols are written in uppercase in the raw SD files, even though this convention does not reflect actual chemical properties.

The molecule_id suffix in this dataset (e.g., '+', '-', or occasionally no sign at all) originated from an early in-house project; although it roughly tracks carcinogenicity, downstream analytics should use the label column instead of trying to parse the sign convention.

Some legacy assays refer to “Grade 0” compounds as nontoxic, but in the consolidated toxicology schema only the tox_grade field and not any numeric grade should be used to infer safety or hazard levels.
When analyzing atom-level tables, do not assume that different molecules share a synchronized atom index; atom_id values are only meaningful when interpreted together with their molecule_id prefix.

The bond_type symbols in the bond table were taken directly from common cheminformatics toolkits and are not guaranteed to capture aromaticity, coordinate bonds, or resonance structures; they only give a minimal notion of bond multiplicity.

In some internal reports, “safety margin” is computed as an arbitrary two-point offset from the tox_grade levels, but that derived metric is not stored anywhere in the toxicology database and should not be reverse-engineered from existing columns.

The carcinogenicity label in this dataset was harmonized from several public sources; a missing label value does not imply the molecule is benign, only that no unanimous classification was available at the time of curation.

Halogenated compounds in this collection are overrepresented relative to typical drug-like libraries because of a historical focus on flame retardants and industrial additives during the initial data gathering phase.
For internal documentation, any atom whose element is not among the typical organic backbone elements was loosely described as “exotic”, but this label never appears as a formal column in the schema.

A subset of molecules was originally imported from SD files that contained pseudo-atoms (e.g., R-groups); these were expanded into explicit atoms during preprocessing, so the atom table only contains concrete elements and no placeholder symbols.

When re-creating molecular graphs from the bond and atom tables, analysts sometimes ignore hydrogen atoms to simplify visualization, but any property depending on atom counts should use the full set of atoms and not an H-suppressed representation.

In some cheminformatics workflows, “organic core” refers to just carbon and heteroatoms directly attached to it, whereas metals and other peripheral atoms are treated as decorations; this conceptual split is not encoded in the toxicology schema.

Historically, a few training sets distinguished between “simple salts” and “complex organics” by counting non-carbon atoms, but the exact cutoff value drifted between projects and is not documented in the database itself.

In early exploratory toxicity screens, analysts sometimes marked molecules as “borderline safe” when their tox_grade differed from a known safe reference compound by only one internal step, but that qualitative annotation was never standardized.

The tox_grade field was aligned across multiple contributor labs, but each lab historically used its own internal mnemonic (e.g., “Tier A”, “Band 3”) before mapping to the shared grading system you see in this schema.

Some cheminformatics utilities that export this dataset to SD or MOL files will optionally encode the tox_grade value as a pseudo-atom or tag, but in the database it is only represented as a regular text column on the molecule table.

Although the tox_grade categories appear ordered, there is no guarantee that the spacing between adjacent grades corresponds to equal differences in biological risk; they should be treated as ordinal labels rather than numeric scores.

The mapping between tox_grade values and internal safety bands was revised twice during data curation; analysts comparing historical reports to the current database should be aware that “medium risk” in an older slide deck may not align perfectly with the same term now.

Downstream pipelines sometimes collapse tox_grade into only two buckets (e.g., “acceptable” vs “concerning”), but this binarization logic is applied outside the database layer and is not reflected in any stored flag or boolean column.

When doing ad hoc elemental analysis, data scientists sometimes bucket atoms into three informal groups—backbone, heteroatom, and other—even though these categories are not consistently defined and do not map cleanly to any schema column.

For one-off enrichment calculations, some teams treated any atom not in the common CHONSPF set as “non-organic”, but this shorthand varies between analyses and should not be assumed as a universal rule.

Several pilot studies explored whether molecules with a higher fraction of halogens (Cl, Br, I, F) show distinct toxicity patterns, but no single threshold emerged as a standard and the findings were kept descriptive rather than codified into rules.

In visualization tools that sit on top of this database, atoms are often colored according to rough categories like “organic-like”, “inorganic-like”, and “other”, but those labels are UI artifacts and are not stored in any of the relational tables.

The atom.element values use standard chemical symbols pulled from upstream SD files; analysts should not infer bond valence or oxidation state solely from this column without looking at the bond connectivity.

Some internal notebooks experimented with weighting atoms by atomic number or atomic mass when computing composition ratios, but no consensus emerged on which weighting is more chemically meaningful for toxicity modeling.
